author: m.kalinin
content: what can go wrong if bids are allowed to be submitted by any version of a builer? how can this be exploited?

author: tersec
content: this entire exercise of planning for an entirely abstract maybe-what-if and adding this complexity when it's currently useless new potential attack surface at best is questionable. If we want to plan for (to use <@592004585506340885>'s example) mandatory proofs, fine, that's worth working out the logistics of. But <@755590043632140352>'s notion of, just plan in an open handwavy way for whatever, let's just see what happens, remains dubious

author: tersec
content: Ethereum already has enough never-used jank

author: tersec
content: to give an example of how that changes reasoning: really concretely, how likely are mandatory proofs in specifically Hegota? I'd currently rank it as on the... low side. Which means we have plenty of time, as Hegotá develops, to watch how I* takes form, and see if there really is something in I* which needs this kind of anticipation. But aim at known targets, not the mist

author: vladimir_ea
content:
It makes sense if the builder trusts the proposer, if they don't rely on trust, the builder would do well to observe p2p instead. 

Also protecting against 'same slot' unbundling is somewhat meaningless, as I am sure you are aware transactions are not tied to slots and any transaction released in slot N that does not land on chain can be applied unbundled in slot N+1.

author: potuz
content: There's no trust here? If the builder gets a signed block at second 1 and broadcast it itself and hasn't seen an equivocation at second 3, looking at attestations is overkill. And yes, builders that want to be protected and not leak any info unless they're sure the block is canonical will need to wait for attestations. This was always the case

author: vladimir_ea
content:
so:
low bar: receive the signed block and then observe that the same block is the only one on p2p
high bar: consume attestations to ensure block is canonical
?

author: potuz
content: Yes, equivocations should always be seen cause they could have been broadcast earlier, the builder is guaranteed not to pay if an equivocation was seen. Attestations are stronger,.

author: huaifeng0666
content:
Hi <@755590043632140352> , our internal AI auditing tool flagged a potential issue in Prysm’s ePBS implementation. We did an initial pass over the report, and the finding looks plausible, but I’d like to get your view on it.

The potential impact could be a consensus split if the report is correct. Full write-up here:
https://notes.ethereum.org/ewmJoTTGRQGUCfR2T8G10w (ethereum account sign-in required)

author: potuz
content: well, get me an ethereum account or open it

author: huaifeng0666
content: Oh sorry my bad! Do you have access to this: https://github.com/ethereum-bounty/prysm/issues/22

author: potuz
content: cc <@363800010518822915> you'll be faster on this one, I think it's your code

author: potuz
content: I spent only a couple of minutes <@1523672392646393936> and it seems invalid to me, the map is built from the current state, I do not believe you can evict a pubkey and reuse it in the same run as this attack seems to need. Anyway I'll let Terence check this cause he's the one that wrote all of this and will know it better, but if I understand correctly the issue, index reuse cannot be triggered in that path.

author: jtraglia
content: By the way, anything specific to Glamsterdam can be shared publicly.
